source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure Network Security is Enabled

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
input_row.title: Ensure Network Security is Enabled
input_row.description: In order to access Amazon Keyspaces the user is required to set specific networking parameters and security measurements without these extra steps they will not be able to access it. Users are required to create or select a virtual private cloud (VPC) and define their inbound and outbound rules accordingly.
input_row.rationale: Impact: Only authorized users have access to the database which limits and controls any risk of an attack. This ensures better performance of the system to a private network and better security.
input_row.audit: 1. Create or Select a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) • Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the Amazon VPC console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/. • Create a new VPC or select an existing VPC where you want to deploy your Amazon Keyspaces resources. 2. Configure Subnets • In the VPC console, navigate to Subnets in the left-side menu. • Create or select the subnets within your VPC where you want to deploy your Amazon Keyspaces resources. • Ensure you have private subnets to isolate your Keyspaces resources from the public internet. 3. Define Security Groups • In the VPC console, navigate to Security Groups in the left-side menu. • Create a new security group or select an existing one for your Amazon Keyspaces resources. • Configure inbound and outbound rules in the security group to control traffic access. o Allow inbound access only from trusted sources, such as specific IP ranges or security groups, on the necessary ports used by Amazon Keyspaces.  o Define outbound rules based on your requirements, allowing outbound traffic to necessary destinations or ports. • Associate the security group with your Amazon Keyspaces resources. 4. Configure Network Access Control Lists (ACLs) • In the VPC console, navigate to Network ACLs in the left-side menu. • Create or select the network ACLs associated with the subnets used by your Amazon Keyspaces resources. • Configure inbound and outbound rules in the network ACLs to control traffic access. o Define rules based on your security requirements, allowing only necessary protocols, ports, and IP ranges. o list text hereDeny unnecessary or unwanted traffic. • Associate the network ACLs with the subnets used by your Amazon Keyspaces resources. 5. Configure VPC Endpoints • In the VPC console, navigate to Endpoints in the left-side menu. • Create or select the VPC endpoints required for Amazon Keyspaces. • If you need to access Keyspaces from within your VPC, create a VPC endpoint for Amazon Keyspaces to connect your applications securely. • If you need to access Keyspaces from another VPC or on-premises network, set up VPC peering or a transit gateway to establish a secure connection. 6. Verify Connectivity and Test • Launch an Amazon EC2 instance within the same VPC and subnet as your Amazon Keyspaces resources or use an existing one. • Connect to the EC2 instance using SSH or other remote access methods. • Test the connectivity to your Amazon Keyspaces resources by trying to connect to them using the appropriate client or utility. • Verify that the network security settings allow the necessary traffic and deny unauthorized access.
input_row.impact: Only authorized users have access to the database which limits and controls any risk of an attack. This ensures better performance of the system to a private network and better security.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control involves several aspects of VPC configuration, such as the setup and verification of VPC, subnets, security groups, network ACLs, and VPC endpoints. Most of these can be verified using AWS APIs or the boto3 SDK. Here’s why this is an automated approach:

- **AWS APIs Availability:** Each component involved (VPC, security groups, network ACLs, VPC endpoints) can be queried and verified using AWS APIs or boto3 SDK.
- **Standard AWS Service Properties:** The control involves common network security configurations in AWS services such as VPC, which are standard and can be checked automatically.
- **boto3 SDK Capability:** boto3 provides sufficient methods to interact with AWS VPC configurations, security groups, network ACLs, and endpoints.
- **No Need for SSH/RDP Access:** This control does not require access to operating systems or any form of manual inspection beyond what's available via AWS configuration.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_vpc_keyspaces_network_security_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS API Calls:**
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2')` to describe and verify VPC configurations.
  - Verify that there are associated subnets with the VPC using `describe_subnets()`.
  - Check that security groups are configured correctly using `describe_security_groups()`, ensuring inbound/outbound rules limit access as specified.
  - Verify network ACLs with `describe_network_acls()`, checking for correct inbound/outbound rules.
  - Check VPC endpoints with `describe_vpc_endpoints()`, ensuring the necessary endpoints for Amazon Keyspaces exist.

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:** 
  - Evaluate VPC, subnets, security groups, network ACLs, and endpoints in all relevant AWS regions where Amazon Keyspaces is deployed.

- **Exact Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
  - Subnets should be private and appropriately associated with a relevant VPC.
  - Security groups should restrict inbound and outbound access appropriately, only permitting traffic from trusted sources.
  - Network ACLs should have applicable rules that deny unnecessary traffic and only allow necessary protocols and IP ranges.
  - Ensure VPC endpoints for Keyspaces exist, verifying private connectivity is enabled.

- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - Pass: All network components (VPC, subnets, security groups, network ACLs, endpoints) are correctly configured.
  - Fail: Any misconfiguration or missing security component as outlined above.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The control involves checking configurations that are accessible and verifiable through AWS APIs, boto3, and AWS CLI. The use of these tools aligns with the capabilities of automated checks.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The boto3 methods mentioned (`describe_vpcs`, `describe_subnets`, `describe_security_groups`, `describe_network_acls`, `describe_vpc_endpoints`) are valid and appropriate for the tasks described.
- The program name, `aws_vpc_keyspaces_network_security_enabled`, is somewhat descriptive but could be improved for clarity and specificity regarding what network security aspects are being checked.
- The description of the checks is comprehensive, but it could benefit from more specific criteria for what constitutes "correct" configuration for security groups and network ACLs.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** Consider renaming to `aws_keyspaces_vpc_network_security_configuration` to better reflect the focus on network security configuration for Amazon Keyspaces.
- **Specific Configuration Checks:** Define more explicit criteria for security group and network ACL rules, such as specifying which ports should be open and to which IP ranges.
- **AWS Config Integration:** Consider integrating AWS Config rules to continuously monitor these configurations for compliance, which would enhance the automation and provide ongoing assurance.
- **Documentation Reference:** Include references to AWS documentation or best practices for network security configurations to guide the specific checks.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of AWS APIs and boto3 is appropriate for the automated approach. The technical details are mostly accurate, and the improvements are minor.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Update the program name for clarity and specificity. Enhance the criteria for evaluating security group and network ACL configurations to ensure they meet best practices. Consider integrating AWS Config for continuous compliance monitoring.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that all required network security configurations for Amazon Keyspaces—VPC existence, subnet assignment, security group rules, and network ACLs—are fully accessible and verifiable via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI, AWS Config). No manual inspection or OS-level access is required; all relevant properties are exposed through standard AWS SDK calls. The only improvement is to use a more precise program name reflecting the specific resource and intent.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_keyspaces_vpc_network_security_configuration

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: Amazon Keyspaces, VPC, Subnets, Security Groups, Network ACLs
- API/CLI Method:
  - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_vpcs()`
  - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_subnets()`
  - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()`
  - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_network_acls()`
  - (Optionally) `boto3.client('keyspaces').list_keyspaces()` to map Keyspaces resources to VPCs
- Properties to Check:
  - Keyspaces resources are associated with a VPC (not public endpoints)
  - Subnets used are private (no direct route to Internet Gateway)
  - Security groups restrict inbound/outbound access to only authorized IP ranges and required ports (e.g., TCP/9142 for Cassandra)
  - Network ACLs do not allow unrestricted access (e.g., 0.0.0.0/0) on sensitive ports
- Pass Criteria:
  - All Keyspaces resources are deployed in a VPC with private subnets
  - Security groups and network ACLs restrict access to only approved sources and ports
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any Keyspaces resource is accessible from public networks, or security groups/network ACLs allow broad/unrestricted access

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation. The second opinion suggested a more descriptive program name and more explicit configuration checks, which have been incorporated for clarity and precision.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
